articles.title: States defang housing opponents, but NIMBYs claw back
articles.narrative: This article discusses the ongoing legislative battles in states like Florida, California, and New York, where state governments are attempting to streamline housing development by curtailing local obstructionism, often referred to as 'NIMBYism'. Despite state-level efforts to expedite projects, local opposition groups continue to employ various tactics, including environmental concerns and even the presence of wildlife, to delay or halt developments. The piece highlights a specific case in Woodland Hills, California, where a developer's plan to convert a golf course into a residential community is facing significant local resistance, illustrating the challenges in overcoming entrenched 'not in my backyard' sentiments.

data_points.project_notes: The site was previously a golf course.

articles.title: Kathie Lee Gifford’s headline listing and Greenwich’s lux surge
articles.narrative: Kathie Lee Gifford has listed her waterfront estate in Riverside, CT, for $100 million, a price point that would make it one of the most expensive residential sales in Greenwich if achieved. While the property boasts significant acreage and water access, some market observers believe the asking price is aspirational and the property may trade closer to $40 million, especially given the likelihood of a teardown. The listing comes amidst a surge in Greenwich's luxury market, which has seen a significant increase in high-value home sales and a corresponding depletion of inventory.

data_points.notable_features: Waterfront estate with views of Long Island Sound and the Manhattan skyline, includes a pier, dock, and private beach.
data_points.project_notes: Property was purchased by Kathie Lee Gifford and Frank Gifford in the early 1990s.

articles.market_intelligence: Greenwich's luxury market has seen 39 homes trade for over $10 million last year, nearly double the previous year. Current inventory is significantly low, with only 100-105 homes on the market compared to 600-700 pre-pandemic. The Upper East Side townhouse market recorded nearly $283 million in deals during Q1 2026, an 80% increase year-over-year, with an average sale price of $16.6 million.

articles.title: Hotel union deal puts NYC owners on notice
articles.narrative: New York City's hotel industry has reached a tentative eight-year labor agreement with the Hotel and Gaming Trades Council, covering nearly 30,000 workers across more than 250 properties. The deal, which is expected to be ratified, includes significant wage increases, with housekeepers projected to earn over $100,000 annually by the sixth year. While the agreement averts a strike ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, hotel owners are concerned about the substantial increase in payroll obligations, which they believe will impact their bottom line amidst rising inflation, tariffs, and borrowing costs. Operators are expected to attempt to raise room rates to offset these increased expenses.

articles.title: The Daily Dirt: Hochul’s budget delays keep NYC real estate in flux
articles.narrative: Governor Kathy Hochul's delayed state budget rollout has created uncertainty in New York City's real estate market, particularly concerning two proposed taxes. The budget, which includes a controversial 1% tax on all-cash home sales over $1 million and a 'pied-à-terre' tax on second homes valued at $5 million or more annually, has yet to be fully approved. Industry players are frustrated by the lack of clarity and the potential for these taxes to negatively impact sales and fail to meet revenue projections. The delay is seen as a strategic move by the governor to pressure lawmakers into accepting her proposals.

data_points.address: 499 Grand Street, New York City, NY

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: UES co-op sells for $13M
articles.narrative: A mixed-use property in Williamsburg, featuring ground-floor retail and upper-level apartments, was the priciest commercial transaction recorded in New York City, trading for $7.5 million. The three-story building at 499 Grand Street was sold by an LLC associated with Greenbrook Partners to Townhouse Rental II, LLC. This transaction marks a significant increase from its previous sale price of $3.8 million in 2024.

data_points.address: 499 Grand Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Three-story building with ground-floor retail and apartments on upper levels.
data_points.project_notes: Property last sold in 2024 for $3.8 million.

articles.title: Hoping to spur development, San Francisco wants to eliminate some affordable housing requirements
articles.narrative: San Francisco city leaders are proposing to reduce the required percentage of affordable housing units in new developments from 15% to 5% to stimulate housing construction, a move that would make the city's on-site affordable housing requirements among the lowest nationally. Projects with 25 or fewer units would be entirely exempt. This proposal, supported by Mayor Daniel Lurie and Supervisor Myrna Melgar, aims to address the city's struggle to build new housing despite a tech boom. Concurrently, a ballot initiative will seek to increase the city's annual contribution to its Housing Trust Fund from $52 million to $125 million to finance and preserve affordable units.

data_points.project_notes: The state of California mandates San Francisco permit at least 45,000 new subsidized housing units by 2031.
